caveats: a: BLS occupation evidence covers all sound-recording industries rather than 512290.

a: Speech-transcription exposure may not represent music, live-event, or broadcast-program workflows.

rho: Client policies may prohibit cloud processing even where the workflow is technically feasible.

d5: BLS covers the entire 512200 group and provides no separate 512290 projection.
d5: The residual industry's service-line weights and historical revenue series are not available in the cited evidence.
d5: Demand for more recorded content can coexist with lower spending per recorded hour.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Revenue is primarily project, event, program, retainer, or managed-service fees paid by conference organizers, institutions, broadcasters, producers, and corporate clients. Work combines capture planning, equipment and crew deployment, live quality assurance, editing, mastering, delivery, archiving, and customer coordination.
lens.excluded_activities: Record labels and distributors, music publishers, sound-recording studios, motion-picture audio postproduction classified elsewhere, mass duplication, event promotion, self-employed creators classified as independent artists, captive internal audiovisual teams, one-off shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the approximate $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market firms in NAICS 512290 that repeatedly provide external customers with nonstudio sound-recording services, including on-location recording of meetings and conferences and production of recorded radio programs where classified in this industry.

a.bridge: BLS reports the largest occupations for the broader NAICS 512200 sound-recording industry, led by sound engineering technicians and producers/directors, alongside managers, marketing specialists, sales, bookkeeping, and office work. Its occupational handbook describes recording, synchronization, format conversion, equipment operation, quality control, repairs, recordkeeping, and client collaboration. I map editing, cleanup, transcription support, file preparation, documentation, scheduling, quoting, reporting, and administration into a wage-weighted AI-exposed share for 512290, while excluding physical setup, live monitoring, troubleshooting, and accountable final quality control. The broad mix may overstate exposure if 512290 is more field-technical than labels and studios, but may understate it where meeting and radio-program workflows contain repetitive speech editing and metadata work.
caveats: BLS occupation evidence covers all sound-recording industries rather than 512290.
The residual NAICS category is poorly described beyond meeting and conference recording, so service-line weights are unknown.
Speech-transcription exposure may not represent music, live-event, or broadcast-program workflows.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task survey of 512290 field crews, editors, producers, coordinators, and administrators that measures current software automation and remaining hours by meeting, conference, radio-program, and other service lines.
a.rationale: Speech cleanup, rough editing, indexing, summaries, transcripts, file naming, quality-flag detection, scheduling, proposals, and customer communications are exposed to AI assistance or avoided hiring. Microphone placement, cabling, live capture, equipment reliability, acoustic judgment, consent handling, troubleshooting, and final delivery assurance remain operator-intensive, and existing digital editing automation is not counted again.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for broad NAICS 512200 sound recording from $31.4 billion in 2024 to $45.1 billion in 2034, a 3.7% annual rate that would imply about 1.20 over five years. I reduce the 512290 midpoint to 1.04 because the broad series likely reflects record production, distribution, publishing, and studio activity far more than the residual meeting, conference, and recorded-program services, and because automated capture can suppress demand for the current service basket. The proxy may overstate this niche if self-recording spreads quickly, or understate it if growth in conferences, podcasts, accessibility, compliance, and archived media expands professional recording demand.
caveats: BLS covers the entire 512200 group and provides no separate 512290 projection.
The residual industry's service-line weights and historical revenue series are not available in the cited evidence.
Demand for more recorded content can coexist with lower spending per recorded hour.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted five-year revenue and recorded-hour indexes for 512290 firms by meeting, conference, radio-program, live-location, and other services, supplemented by event-volume and customer self-capture adoption data.
d5.rationale: Growth in recorded events, podcasts, remote participation, searchable archives, accessibility, and content reuse can support service quantity, but ubiquitous conferencing tools and customer-owned capture systems reduce outsourced assignments. With no six-digit time series, a near-flat base and a range spanning contraction to meaningful growth are more defensible than carrying through the broad forecast.

caveats: The official definition gives few examples, leaving the composition of the residual category uncertain.
No source measures the repeat-service or transferable-firm share in the supplied LMM population.
The supplied n estimate relies on a broad information-services margin that may not fit field recording and contractor-heavy production.
e.change_evidence: A census of LMM 512290 firms classifying customer concentration, repeat revenue, service lines, contract duration, owner dependence, field-crew model, and normalized EBITDA, verified against customer invoices and tax records.
e.rationale: The Census definition is service-based and explicitly identifies audio recording of meetings and conferences, which commonly supports repeat institutional and event customers. Eligibility is reduced by one-off project shops, owner-technician dependence, radio-program or other residual activities without repeat outsourced demand, captive units, and firms whose EBITDA-band classification is distorted by equipment, contractor, or project economics. The supplied n already estimates the size band.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 512290 ($233,021,640) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 512290 ($514,773,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1728 (margins.json[51]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Information Services EBITDA/Sales 17.28% (conservative sector default)); revenue/employee $219,613 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 512290); class <5: 461 firms, avg revenue/firm $313,232 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $54,127 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 46 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,114,848 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $192,646 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 23 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,000,087 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $518,415 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 18 firms, avg revenue/firm $7,845,222 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $1,355,654 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 3 firms, avg revenue/firm $13,551,000 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,341,613 -> IN $1-10M band; n_band = 21

caveats: No source measures the future operator-required share for 512290.
The split between simple meeting capture and technically demanding field production is unknown.
Some automation may change the operator's workflow rather than eliminate the accountable service provider.
o.change_evidence: Customer cohort data measuring migration of outsourced recording hours to built-in conferencing capture, automated production, internal audiovisual teams, and AI-only postprocessing, segmented by event complexity, confidentiality, and required quality.
o.rationale: Complex live and on-location assignments still require accountable planning, consent handling, microphone and equipment setup, real-time monitoring, troubleshooting, secure transfer, and final quality assurance. Standard meetings, clean speech, routine radio segments, transcription, and basic edits can increasingly be self-recorded or automated through conferencing platforms and AI tools, so a material portion of current-basket quantity may no longer require a specialist operator within five years.

caveats: No source measures pricing structure or benefit sharing for 512290.
Retention differs between fixed-deliverable, hourly, crew-day, retainer, and bundled event contracts.
Customer savings from self-service belong in operator-required demand rather than being counted again here.
q.change_evidence: Job-level panels linking AI-assisted labor hours to bids, realized prices, contract renewals, customer churn, subcontractor costs, turnaround commitments, and gross margin across comparable recording assignments.
q.rationale: Project and managed-service pricing can let an operator retain near-term efficiency gains when deliverables and quality are fixed rather than labor reimbursed. Retention erodes as clients benchmark cheaper automated transcription and self-recording options, hourly or crew-day pricing exposes lower effort, competitive bids reset, and operators reinvest in quality assurance, security, faster turnaround, or new services.

caveats: No source reports AI implementation or realized labor savings for 512290.
Performance on clean recorded speech can materially overstate performance in live, noisy, confidential, or multi-speaker settings.
Client policies may prohibit cloud processing even where the workflow is technically feasible.
rho.change_evidence: Vendor and operator cohorts for nonstudio recording businesses reporting active AI use, labor hours per recorded hour, word and speaker-attribution error rates, rework, customer acceptance, privacy incidents, and retained deployment by service line.
rho.rationale: Exposed post-capture and office workflows can be added to common digital-audio and business systems within five years. Realized implementation is reduced by noisy or multi-speaker audio, terminology and accent errors, customer confidentiality, consent and retention rules, interoperability, uneven file practices, live-event failure costs, and the need for human listening and sign-off.

s5.bridge: Gallup's fall-2024 survey reports five-year sell or transfer intentions of 22% among US employer-business owners and 9% among nonemployers. I place the 512290 base between those benchmarks but closer to the nonemployer result because many technical-service firms may depend on an owner-producer, customer relationships, or personal operating expertise; I also discount stated plans for noncompletion and nonqualifying transfers. This can overstate completed deals because intentions are not closings, while it can understate unsolicited acquisitions of recurring client books and trained crews.
caveats: Gallup is not specific to the industry, size band, or technical-service business model.
Intentions do not measure completed qualifying control transfers.
The employer-versus-nonemployer mix of the estimated LMM population is not observed.
s5.change_evidence: Closed business-sale and control-transfer records for nonstudio audio recording, conference recording, and recorded-program producers, paired with an eligible-firm denominator and post-close customer-retention outcomes over five-year cohorts.
s5.rationale: Repeat client contracts, equipment, workflow know-how, archives, and trained crews can transfer, but the residual category is small and likely contains founder-led technical shops that close or lose clients when the owner exits. Broad intentions therefore justify only a modest five-year qualifying-transfer rate.

principal_driver: Repeat event and program workflows contain substantial digital post-capture work while complex assignments still value accountable technical delivery.
principal_weakness: The residual industry's composition is poorly observed, and routine meeting recording faces direct substitution from customer-owned and automated tools.

sources.supports: Official 512290 definition, meeting and conference recording example, exclusions, and service-model lens.
